issue: Quantitative and qualitative expansion of nuclear arsenals

entries.position_summary: The country supports negotiating a Fissile Material Cut-Off Treaty in Geneva. It views such a treaty as a necessary step to prevent future nuclear arms racing.
entries.quote: And, here in Geneva, we continue to advocate for the negotiation of a Fissile Material Cut-Off Treaty, a step needed to help stop future arms racing in its tracks.

entries.position_summary: The delegation condemns Syria’s safeguards non-compliance, the DPRK’s unchecked nuclear and ballistic advances, and Russia’s seizure of Ukraine’s Zaporizhzhia NPP, urging accountability for actions undermining the nonproliferation regime. It criticizes Russia and the PRC for expanding and diversifying their nuclear arsenals while showing little interest in arms control, and warns about destabilizing coordination among Russia, the PRC, the DPRK, and Iran.
entries.quote: agreement, Syria’s non-compliance with its comprehensive safeguards agreement, the DPRK’s unchecked advances of its nuclear and ballistic weapons programs, and Russia’s seizure of Ukraine’s Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant. Furthermore, Russia and the PRC are expanding and diversifying their nuclear weapons arsenals — showing little or no interest in arms control. Russia, the PRC, and the DPRK, together with Iran, are increasingly cooperating and coordinating with each other in ways that run counter to peace and stability and exacerbate regional tensions. We will have more to say on these topics in our statement on regional issues; however, my delegation wishes to stress that part of keeping the nonproliferation regime strong is holding those whose actions contradict it accountable.

entries.position_summary: The country asserts that it is not the United States but another state that is undertaking a large peacetime nuclear buildup. It criticizes the minimal transparency surrounding that buildup, implying concern over unchecked arsenal expansion and opacity.
entries.quote: It is also not the United States that is actively engaging in one of the largest peacetime nuclear build-ups in history, with minimal transparency into those activities.

entries.position_summary: The country asserts that China is rapidly expanding its nuclear weapons capabilities and doing so opaquely. This reflects concern over both the growth of China’s arsenal and its lack of transparency.
entries.quote: The People’s Republic of China (PRC) continues a rapid and opaque expansion of its nuclear weapons capabilities.

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes high transparency standards for its nuclear policies and supports incorporating structured dialogue, such as national reports, into the NPT review process, regretting that this was blocked. It calls for the urgent negotiation of a Fissile Material Cutoff Treaty to prevent a renewed nuclear arms race and lay groundwork for further progress.
entries.quote: It is why the United States prioritizes high standards for transparency of our nuclear policies and programs. We welcome discussion on our national report, and deeply regret that certain parties prevented the Working Group from adopting such structured dialogue a regular part of the NPT review process.] It is also why we believe the time has come for a long overdue – and urgently needed – Fissile Material Cutoff Treaty. Without it, we risk a return to an era of nuclear arms racing. With it, we can set a better foundation for progress on

entries.position_summary: The statement criticizes China’s rapid, opaque nuclear buildup and rejects China’s proposed No First Use treaty as non-credible and unverifiable. It also condemns Russia’s coercive nuclear rhetoric and highlights concern over Russia’s increased military cooperation with the DPRK.
entries.quote: Such falsehoods distract from the truly worrisome issues at hand, including China’s rapid and opaque nuclear weapons build-up, and Russia’s coercive nuclear rhetoric and increased military cooperation with the DPRK. We were dismayed by some States Parties’ support for China’s proposal for a non- credible, unverifiable No First Use Treaty. We must ask – what good is a statement from China that it will not use nuclear weapons first, when its nuclear build-up appears to contradict to its stated policy?

entries.position_summary: The statement condemns Russia for qualitatively expanding its nuclear forces, undermining arms control by purporting to suspend New START, and engaging in irresponsible nuclear rhetoric. It also accuses Russia of violating its security assurances to Ukraine.
entries.quote: Russia has developed novel nuclear weapons and improved dual-capable delivery systems, unlawfully purported to suspend the New START Treaty, engaged in irresponsible nuclear rhetoric, and reneged on the security assurances it provided Ukraine.

entries.position_summary: The United States criticizes China’s rapid, opaque expansion of its nuclear arsenal, citing new ICBM silos, delivery systems, and ongoing production of fissile material, with projections of 1,000+ warheads by 2030. It argues China’s buildup contradicts its stated policies of minimum deterrence and No First Use and contrasts with other NPT nuclear-weapon states’ behavior. The U.S. asserts it acts responsibly as a nuclear-weapon state under the NPT.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, the United States is committed to responsible behavior as a nuclear-weapon State under the NPT. Not all NPT nuclear-weapon States have done the same. We continue to be deeply concerned by China’s rapid, opaque, and destabilizing expansion of its nuclear arsenal, including the construction of hundreds of new ICBM silos and the development of new delivery systems. We assess that China currently has 600 operational nuclear weapons, which will increase to over 1,000 by the year 2030, and that it is actively producing new fissile material for use in its growing nuclear arsenal, unlike the other four nuclear-weapon states. China’s activities indicate it seeks the ability to inflict far greater levels of overwhelming damage to an adversary in a nuclear exchange than it has historically deemed necessary, and all while professing to apply “minimum deterrence” and “No First Use” in its nuclear authorities.

entries.position_summary: The statement criticizes China’s rapid and opaque nuclear build-up, questioning the credibility and clarity of its no-first-use policy and broader doctrine. It notes Beijing’s reluctance to engage substantively and expresses skepticism about a proposed no-first-use treaty, particularly regarding how it would be verified. Overall, it views China’s initiative as lacking substance.
entries.quote: The PRC’s actions, in particular its rapid and opaque nuclear weapons build-up, raise questions about Beijing’s already ambiguous, stated “no first use” policy and its nuclear doctrine more broadly, calling into question what such an initiative aims to achieve. The PRC, to date, has resisted substantive engagement to answer these questions. We also continue to have concerns about how its proposed no first use treaty would operate in practice, including with respect to verification. We see more style than substance.

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes China for rapidly and opaquely expanding its nuclear arsenal and warns it could surpass 1,000 warheads by 2030, posing a threat to global peace and stability.
entries.quote: As we speak, China is rapidly and opaquely building up its nuclear arsenal, threatening global peace and stability. We assess that Beijing will exceed 1,000 nuclear warheads by the year 2030.

entries.position_summary: The statement criticizes China for rapidly expanding its nuclear arsenal while avoiding engagement, and questions whether China is committed to nuclear restraint and meeting its Article VI disarmament obligations. It implies concern that China's behavior undermines progress toward disarmament.
entries.quote: • And the People’s Republic of China is rapidly expanding its nuclear arsenal while avoiding engagement. Given the circumstance, it is reasonable to ask whether the PRC genuinely shares a commitment to nuclear restraint and the NPT’s Article VI goals.

entries.position_summary: The United States expresses concern about the growth of nuclear arsenals and capabilities in Asia among both NPT Parties and non-Parties. It urges all nuclear-armed states to exercise restraint and to establish and maintain moratoria on the production of fissile material for nuclear weapons.
entries.quote: Finally, the United States remains concerned by the growth of nuclear weapons stockpiles and capabilities of NPT Parties and non-Parties in Asia, and we continue to encourage all states with nuclear weapons to exercise restraint regarding their nuclear weapons and missile capabilities, including by establishing and maintaining moratoria on production of fissile material for use in nuclear weapons.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms strong support for the NPT and Article VI, highlighting an 88% reduction of its nuclear arsenal, dismantlement of thousands of warheads, and elimination of entire classes of delivery systems. It also notes a moratorium of over 30 years on producing fissile material for nuclear weapons.
entries.quote: The United States remains a strong supporter of the NPT, including the disarmament-related obligations contained in Article VI, and we have taken significant steps in this area, reducing U.S. nuclear arsenal by 88 percent, dismantling thousands of warheads, and eliminating entire classes of nuclear delivery systems. For more than 30 years, we have implemented a moratorium on the production of fissile material for use in nuclear weapons or other nuclear explosive devices. These are effective measures that 1

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes China for expanding its nuclear arsenal while withholding basic information on stockpiles, fissile materials, and nuclear posture. It argues China’s lack of transparency and refusal to join arms control efforts undermines its commitment to NPT objectives and the broader nonproliferation regime.
entries.quote: Meanwhile, China refuses to disclose even the most basic information about its growing stockpile size, fissile material holdings and production, or to answer basic questions regarding its nuclear posture. Its actions, lack of transparency, and especially its refusal to join arms control efforts raise serious questions about its commitment to the NPT’s objectives, undermining the global nonproliferation regime.

entries.position_summary: The country reports an 88% reduction of its nuclear stockpile and seeks to further lessen the role of nuclear weapons in its defense strategy, while acknowledging current security conditions. This frames its actions as contributing to disarmament and provides a degree of transparency.
entries.quote: We have reduced the number of nuclear weapons in our stockpile by over 88% and, while fully cognizant of the current security environment, continue to seek opportunities to reduce the role of nuclear weapons in our national defense strategy.

entries.position_summary: The United States urges the P5 to pursue multilateral arms control and risk-reduction measures to prevent nuclear war and avoid arms races. As P5 Chair, it convened multiple expert-level meetings in 2023 to advance these efforts.
entries.quote: In parallel, the five nuclear weapons states must continue to engage in multilateral arms control and risk reduction efforts; they have an urgent responsibility to work together to prevent nuclear war and avoid arms races. As the P5 Chair, the United States has held multiple expert level meetings in 2023 to that end.

entries.position_summary: The statement criticizes China for expanding its nuclear forces in ways seen as inconsistent with its stated No First Use policy and traditional minimum deterrent posture. It urges China to increase transparency and engage in arms control by answering outstanding questions at the PrepCom.
entries.quote: stated “No First Use” policy and strays from its historic focus on a “minimum deterrent” force structure. China, to date, has resisted calls to explain its build-up or engage on arms control. During this PrepCom, we hope China will abandon its approach of distractions and avoidance and instead demonstrate responsibility by answering the questions many of us have posed over the last two PrepComs.

entries.position_summary: The United States argues that Russia and China’s refusal to engage in arms control compels it and its allies to prepare for unconstrained nuclear competition. In such a context, the U.S. may reassess and potentially augment its nuclear capabilities and adjust its posture to address these threats.
entries.quote: However, in contrast to our positive approach and longstanding efforts to manage rivalry and unrestrained competition through arms control, the outright refusal of Russia and the PRC to even discuss arms control at this time obliges the United States and our close allies and partners to prepare for a world of nuclear competition without numerical constraints. In such a world, the United States might have to reconsider its capabilities and posture to account for the threats posed by Russia and the PRC.

entries.position_summary: The country presses Russia to uphold arms control and pursue risk reduction, rejecting Russia’s purported suspension of New START as legally invalid and condemning its nuclear rhetoric for heightening risks of miscalculation. It also criticizes China’s rapid and opaque nuclear buildup, calling into question Beijing’s transparency and intentions.
entries.quote: We continue to press Russia on arms control and risk reduction despite its legally invalid purported suspension of the New START Treaty and its rhetoric regarding nuclear weapons that not only increase nuclear risks but also heighten the possibility of miscalculation. Meanwhile, China’s rapid and opaque nuclear weapons build-up raises questions about Beijing’s 2

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes addressing the threat from Russian and Chinese nuclear arsenals while showcasing its own transparency by publicly releasing stockpile numbers. It criticizes China and Russia for opaque build-ups and failing to reciprocate on transparency.
entries.quote: He has made clear his desire to address the threat posed by Russia and China’s nuclear arsenals. In sharp contrast to China’s opaque nuclear weapons build-up, the United States has been remarkably transparent, releasing the number of weapons in our stockpile twice in the last four years. However, for too long U.S. leadership and transparency have not been met with reciprocity by China and Russia.

entries.position_summary: The country reaffirms commitment to a world without nuclear weapons while warning that Russia, China, and North Korea are rapidly expanding and diversifying their arsenals. It calls for collective pressure on these states to undertake immediate nuclear risk-reduction measures.
entries.quote: Such a step would not reflect an abandonment of our principles or commitment to pursuing the shared goal of a world without nuclear weapons. But we cannot ignore the current security environment in which Russia, the PRC, and the DPRK are all expanding and diversifying their arsenals at breakneck pace. That is why we need your collective voices in demanding that all these countries engage in nuclear risk reduction efforts without further delay.

entries.position_summary: Expresses concern over the expanding nuclear arsenals and delivery systems of India and Pakistan, and urges all nuclear-armed states to exercise restraint in developing nuclear and missile capabilities.
entries.quote: And finally, Chair, we remain concerned by the growing number of nuclear weapons and delivery systems in India and Pakistan and continue to encourage all states with nuclear weapons to exercise restraint regarding nuclear and missile capabilities.

entries.position_summary: The United States urges strengthening the NPT and pursuing Article VI-consistent, engagement-based arms control to prevent nuclear arms races, calling specifically on China and Russia to participate. It signals readiness to engage and critiques what it sees as ‘shortcuts’ to disarmament, implicitly rejecting the TPNW approach ahead of the next Review Conference.
entries.quote: As we look toward next year’s Review Conference, we must remember what brought us here decades ago. We are here to advance our collective security by strengthening the NPT, preventing the proliferation of nuclear weapons, and, yes, pursuing their ultimate elimination. The next step is clear, we need China and Russia to work with us to that end. Unlike some efforts that seek shortcuts to nuclear disarmament, it is only through such engagement that we can pursue effective measures to prevent nuclear arms races and make progress towards nuclear disarmament. The United States remains ready to pursue that way forward, and we urge everyone to use your collective voices to demand that these states engage without further 4

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes Russia for developing novel nuclear weapons and enhancing dual-capable delivery systems, signaling expansion and modernization of its arsenal. It also asserts that Russia has refused to return to compliance with the New START Treaty as it nears expiration, resisting arms control constraints.
entries.quote: Russia is developing novel nuclear weapons and improving its suite of dual- capable delivery systems. It has refused to return to compliance with the New START Treaty, which expires in less than a year, and has resisted 2

entries.position_summary: The United States urges China to engage in arms control talks now as part of its NPT/Article VI obligations, rather than waiting until its arsenal reaches a certain size. It signals concern about the growth of China’s nuclear forces and frames early engagement as a test of Beijing’s NPT commitments.
entries.quote: The United States calls on China to act responsibly and engage in these discussions. If China is serious about its NPT commitments, the world should not have to wait until China’s nuclear program reaches a set number for China to engage seriously in arms control discussions.
